file_name: SPIENERGYCO,LTD_07_10_2014-EX-10-Cooperation Agreement of 50MWp Photovoltaic Grid-connected Power Generation Project in Yangqiao of~1.txt

perturbation.original_text: Party B will register a foreign-funded company in Fenyi County before implementing the project; Party A will offer all the preferential policies to the company as per local investment promotion policies for foreign investment introduction.
perturbation.changed_text: Party B will register a foreign-funded company in Fenyi County within 180 days of signing this agreement. Party A will determine the preferential policies at its sole discretion, and these policies may be altered or revoked without notice.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the registration timeline to a fixed 180 days could potentially conflict with regulations regarding the establishment of foreign-funded companies in China, depending on the specific requirements and processing times. Also, granting Party A the discretion to alter or revoke preferential policies without notice introduces uncertainty and could be deemed unfair or against good faith practices, depending on the specific context and applicable laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Article 8 of the Law of the People's Republic of China on Foreign Investment
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 8 requires that the State ensures foreign-invested enterprises participate in standard-setting activities on an equal footing. The new text introduces an element of uncertainty about preferential policies and changes without notice, possibly creating an unfair playing field. There is no guarantee that these policies comply with the laws and regulations of the People's Republic of China or with international practices. It also does not specify the types of preferential policies offered, nor does it guarantee their duration, thus creating ambiguity.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces uncertainty regarding preferential policies by allowing Party A to alter or revoke them without notice. The Law Explanation states this might create an unfair playing field. This falls under the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where the modification introduces uncertainty, and, while the scraped snippet does not address preferential treatment, the change in the text undermines the original agreement. This inconsistency makes the contract's terms potentially unenforceable, because the duration and existence of preferential policies are now uncertain.

perturbation.original_text: Party B promises that land nature will not be changed for building large-scale PV grid-connected power generation project in the plot; comprehensive development will be carried out according to relevant national stipulations, such commercial crops will be interplanted as agriculture and forestry as well as medicinal materials; local employees will enjoy the priority to be employed; local building materials will be adopted and relevant expenses will be paid on schedule.
perturbation.changed_text: Party B promises that land nature will not be changed. Local employees may be employed at Party B's discretion, prioritizing their own hiring policies above local labor laws. Local building materials will be adopted when feasible.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the firm commitment to prioritize local employees directly contradicts labor laws that may require fair hiring practices and equal opportunities, especially in regions promoting local employment. Also, changing 'will be adopted' to 'will be adopted when feasible' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation to use local building materials, potentially conflicting with local economic development policies.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Labor Law of the People's Republic of China, Article 12
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 12 of the Labor Law of the People's Republic of China mandates that workers shall not be discriminated against in employment because of their ethnic group, race, sex or religious belief. By prioritizing their own hiring policies above local labor laws, the modified text creates the potential for discriminatory practices, potentially conflicting with the Labor Law of the People's Republic of China.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the commitment to local employment, stating that local employees 'may be employed at Party B's discretion, prioritizing their own hiring policies above local labor laws.' This contradicts the original text's firm commitment to 'local employees will enjoy the priority to be employed.' Although the scraped snippets are missing, the Law Explanation states, 'Article 12 of the Labor Law of the People's Republic of China mandates that workers shall not be discriminated against in employment because of their ethnic group, race, sex or religious belief.' Prioritizing their own hiring policies creates the potential for discriminatory practices and conflicts with this Labor Law. Therefore, the changes made to the contract create a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Party A supports and guarantees the project construction and development of Party B, and will provide the most preferential policies and all- round service in the aspects of preliminary work, construction and grid-connected of the project, and actively coordinate relevant departments and units to help Party B accelerate project construction progress.
perturbation.changed_text: Party A will endeavor to support the project construction and development of Party B, but makes no guarantees regarding preferential policies or services. Party A will coordinate departments as deemed necessary, but the timing and extent of such coordination will be at Party A's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the guarantee of preferential policies and all-round service creates uncertainty and could potentially conflict with regulations regarding fair treatment of foreign investments. Making coordination discretionary may also hinder the project's progress, potentially conflicting with policies aimed at promoting renewable energy development.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Foreign Investment Law of the People's Republic of China, Article 4
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 4 of the Foreign Investment Law states that foreign investment shall be encouraged, and the State protects foreign investment in the People’s Republic of China. By removing guarantees, the new text potentially undermines this protection, introducing uncertainty about policy enforcement and support for the project.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: y not compel state governors to surrender fugitives through the issue of writs of mandamus . The Dennison decision was overruled by Puerto Rico v. Branstad (1987); now, the federal courts may require the extradition of fugitives. [ 2 ] Alleged fugitives generally may not challenge extradition proceedings. The motives of the governor demanding the extradition may not be questioned. The accused cannot defend himself against the charges in the extraditing state; the fugitive must do so in the state receiving him. However, the accused may prevent extradition by offering clear evidence that he was not in the state he allegedly fled from at the time of the crime. [ 3 ] There is no constitutional requirement that extradited fugitives be tried only for the crimes named in the extradition proceedings. Fugitives brought to states by means other than extradition may be tried, even though the means of the conveyance was unlawful; the Supreme Court so ruled in Mahon v. Justice , 127 U.S. 700 (1888). In Mahon a body of armed men from Kentucky forcibly took, without a warrant, a man in West Virginia to bring him back to Kentucky for formal arrest and trial. Clause 3: Fugitive Slave Clause [ edit ] Main article: Fugitive Slave Clause No Person held to Service or Labour in one State, under the Laws thereof, escaping into another, shall, in Consequence of any Law or Regulation therein, be discharged from such Service or Labour, but shall be delivered up on Claim of the Party to whom such Service or Labour may be due. Pierce Butler and Charles Pinckney , both from South Carolina , submitted this clause to the Constitutional Convention . James Wilson of Pennsylvania objected, stating it would require that state governments enforce slavery at taxpayers' expense. Butler withdrew the clause. However, on the next day the clause was quietly reinstated and adopted by the Convention without objection. This clause was added to the clause that provided extradition for fugitives from justice. [ 4 ] When first adopted, this clause applied to fugitive slaves and required that they be extradited upon the claims of their masters, but it provided no means for doing so. The Fugitive Slave Act of 1793 created the mechanism for recovering a fugitive slave, overruled any state laws giving sanctuary , made it a federal crime to assist an escaped slave, and allowed slave-catchers into every U.S. state and territory. As free states sought to undermine the federal law, the even more severe Fugitive Slave Act of 1850 was enacted. In 1864, during the Civil War, an effort to repeal this clause of the Constitution failed. The vote in the House was 69 for repeal and 38 against, which was short of the two-to-one vote required to amend the Constitution. [ 5 ] This clause was rendered mostly moot when the Thirteenth Amendment abolished involuntary servitude . Section 3: New states and federal property [ edit ] Clause 1: Admission of new states [ edit ] Main article: Admission to the Union See also: List of U.S. states by date of admission to the Union New States may be admitted by the Congress into this Union; but no new States shall be formed or erected within the Jurisdiction of any other State; nor any State be formed by the Junction of two or more States, or parts of States, without the Consent of the Legislatures of the States concerned as well as of the Congress. The First Clause of Section Three, also known as the Admissions Clause , [ 6 ] grants to Congress the authority to admit new states into the Union. Since the establishment of the United States in 1776, the number of states has expanded from the original 13 to 50. It also forbids the creation of new states from parts of existing states without the consent of the affected states legislature and Congress. perturbation.justification: The original text guarantees support and preferential policies, while the changed text removes these guarantees, making support discretionary. The Law Explanation states that Article 4 of the Foreign Investment Law encourages foreign investment and protects it. By removing guarantees, the changed text potentially undermines this protection, introducing uncertainty about policy enforcement and support for the project, thus contradicting the intent of the law to protect and encourage foreign investment.
